div1.entryFree:
Blunt, adj., 1) having a thick edge, not sharp: thy (love's) edge should --er be than appetite, Sonn. 56, 2. as b. as the fencers' foils, Ado V, 2, 13. R3 IV, 4, 226. Troil. I, 3, 316.
2) dull in understanding: b. Thurio, Gentl. II, 6, 41. of so easy and so plain a stóp that the b. monster with uncounted heads can play upon it, H4B Ind. Gentl. II, 6, 41 Err. IV, 2, 21.
3) rough, regardless, harsh: no gentle chase, but the b. boar, rough bear, or lion proud, Ven. 884. a sharp wit matched with too b. a will: whose edge hath power to cut, whose will still wills it should none spare that come within his power, LLL II, 49. with hasty Germans and b. Hollanders, H6C IV, 8, 2. that Clarence is so harsh, so b., unnatural, to bend the fatal instruments of war against his brother, V, 1, 86. I have too long borne your b. upbraidings, R3 I, 3, 104.
4) plain, unceremonious: a good b. fellow, John I, 71. I judge by his blunt bearing he will keep his word, H5 IV, 7, 185. a plain b. man, Caes. III, 2, 222. (Shr. II, 45 and III, 2, 13?)
5) clumsy, awkward: this is too curious-good, this b. and ill, Lucr. 1300. Lucr. 1300 Sonn. 103, 7. Ado III, 5, 12 (in Dogberry's speech). Merch. II, 7, 8. Shr. II, 45. III, 2, 13. H6B IV, 1, 67. H6C III, 2, 83. Caes. I, 2, 299.*Hml. III, 4, 182 (Qq blowt, M. Edd. bloat). Cymb. V, 5, 325.
